vocab.meaning: imprisoned, captive — irregular p.p. of prender

grammar.title: rogar + que + subjunctive
grammar.detail: ‘les ruego que vivan…’ — verbs of pleading/requesting trigger the subjunctive in the following clause. ‘vivan’ = 3rd plural present subjunctive of vivir.

grammar.title: ‘digno de’ + noun
grammar.detail: ‘worthy of’ — fixed preposition: digno de algo, digno de hacer algo.

expression.phrase: vivir de una manera digna del llamamiento
expression.note: Classic Pauline move: connect identity (‘who you are in Christ’) to behaviour (‘how you walk’). Idiomatic in Spanish too: ‘to live in a way worthy of …’

grammar.title: Adjective stacking with ‘siempre’
grammar.detail: ‘siempre humildes y amables, pacientes, tolerantes…’ Several adjectives in apposition, modifying the implied subject (you, plural). No verb needed — they’re elaborating ‘vivan’ from v.1.

grammar.title: ‘unos con otros’
grammar.detail: ‘with one another’ — Spanish’s standard reciprocal phrase, used after a verb or adjective: aman unos a otros, pacientes unos con otros.

expression.phrase: tolerantes unos con otros en amor
expression.note: ‘bearing with one another in love’ — in modern Spanish ‘tolerante’ is a softer rendering than the older ‘soportar / aguantar’. The point: patience anchored in love, not gritted teeth.

grammar.title: Imperative ‘Esfuércense’
grammar.detail: ustedes imperative of the reflexive esforzarse. Reflexive pronoun attaches to the end: esforzarse → esfuércense. Note the written accent.

grammar.title: ‘por mantener…’
grammar.detail: ‘por + infinitive’ = ‘in order to…’ here. Spanish uses ‘por’ + infinitive for ‘the way / means by which’.

expression.note: ‘the bond of peace’ — Paul imagines peace as a cord that ties believers to each other. The image returns in Col 3:14 (love = the bond of perfection).
